---
title: "I Am So Done with AI Detection Services"
layout: post
tags: [Anthology]
---
![Picture of manuscripts with annotations](/assets/images/AI_detection1.jpg)
##### Image generated by Google Gemini

# The Penance

A few of my friends and my mother know that as soon as I returned from the UK in Autumn 2024, I started a part-time job. I work as a teacher for an institution that specialises in academic tutoring for Chinese students studying abroad. In case you don’t know, this is a grey-industry in fact as all schools assume you are completing your assessment independently or with mostly-controlled assistance. So working in this industry often involves practices that can regarded as academic misconduct.


To be honest, It is not a well-paid job. They charge students on a hourly rate basis, usually around CN¥800-1200 per hour (as you get discount if you purchase by bulk) and I receive less than a quarter of that. Not to mention that 1 hour of online teaching requires hours of preparation that can easily keep your ass glued on a chair from afternoon to evening. Still, it covers some of my expenses at least, considering I only earn a marginal monthly income for someone living in Beijing. But that’s a story for another time.

In my mother’s word, only kids who can’t make it academically would purchase these services, proved she’s right. Their customers are often students with intermediate or limit academic literacies. If you’re lucky, your student might produce a pretty decent, or at least adequate, essay after a one-hour lecture on an outline you built for them. In those cases, it only takes a few minutes to identify minor issues afterwards. Most of the time, however, they simply hand in craps, and it takes a massive effort just to teach them how to properly cite a theorist in Harvard format.

But the most exhausting, nightmarish part is those who purchased "essay polishing" services. This requires you to proofread and modify a student’s work to ensure it won't be flagged by Turnitin’s plagiarism and AI detection services.

# Man vs. Machine

The Turnitin AI detector rates AI suspicion on a percentage system; anything below a certain threshold will marked as a “\*%”, and that is usually 20%. However, the biggest problem with AI detectors is that their results, to be straightforward, are a hit-or-miss. The detection algorithm is essentially a black box; no one, not even Jesus, could possibly predict what happens once you submit an article to their systems.

My recent experience with these detectors has been tiring and confusing. I once took an order from a student at an Australian university where the policy forced her to take an elective course with zero association with her degree program. After I rewrote the majority of her essay and submitted the document to Turnitin, the result came back as 26% AI. Since this was above the threshold, I had to rework to “humanise" the writing. By simply changing a few words to more colloquial ones, it passed effortlessly.

I wasn’t so lucky the next time. A student from a Russell Group university in Northern England (just one hour away from my alma matar), who was taking a course on the music industry, was struggling to produce a blog article. She had zero knowledge of Western music or industrial dynamics. When I asked why she picked the course, she simply said she "liked music," forgetting that she hadn’t even listened to a Taylor Swift track (or maybe she knew "Love Story" at most).

My lecture with her was an absolute nightmare. I spent over 40 minutes just explaining how music copyrights and royalties work. But that was only the beginning. When I asked her to explain "political economy" in her own words, she answered: “Political economy is political economy.” WOW, such an insightful argument. The lecture, which was supposed to take an hour, stretched to almost 80 minutes, and that was only because the system cuts you off after a 20-minute overrun.

A few days later, I received her first draft of the 1,000-word blog. It turned out she hadn't understood even a quarter of what I taught. It looked nothing like an article you’d find on The Conversation. The structure and rationale were more chaotic than a ball of wool that your cat is playing when no one’s around; the assignment was hopeless, and I had to rewrite every single sentence for her.

After spending an entire afternoon and the following morning reworking her gibberish, the AI score increased from 80% to a whopping 100%. I confess I asked Gemini to help improve the grammar, but seriously? 100%? It eventually took five gruelling revisions to finally get that “Unlikely AI” rating on Turnitin.

I have showed Turnitin every possible trick that was worth it: switching to passive voice, breaking long sentences into short ones (even when it made zero sense and disrupted the narrative), replacing formal language with Reddit-style slang, or moving sentences to different paragraphs. It was far worse than editing product FAQs for a smartphone manufacturer back when I worked at a PR agency—at least they were comfortable with AI.

# The Rationale

Reading about my sense of inadequacy and perplexity, you might resonate with me or think the institution was pushing me too hard. But I am just a very small, neglectable molecule in this higher education system. What I want to say is that the implementation of AI detection services is catastrophic. In my opinion, they should never have been invented since day 1. 

To begin with, I think it is important to show you how AI detectors respond to written content. While these are not Turnitin scores, the underlying algorithms across these platforms is very similar. Here is how they "scored" selected media:

| Item          | AI Rate          |
|------------------|------------------|
| An essay I wrote before I realised you could ask GPT to polish your writing (saving me from clicking “accept” a thousand times on Grammarly) | 20% AI |
| Several paragraphs from a book written by my professor before LLMs even existed | 23% AI |
| A section from my own dissertation, which I asked ChatGPT to improve for flow, clarity, and grammar | 26% AI |
| [Apple’s press release for the MacBook Pro with M5 Pro/Max chips](https://www.apple.com/newsroom/2026/03/apple-introduces-macbook-pro-with-all-new-m5-pro-and-m5-max/) | 51% AI |
| [Taylor Swift’s Midnights Album Prologue](https://genius.com/Taylor-swift-midnights-liner-notes-annotated) | 55% AI |


The case of Taylor Swift is very telling. The Midnights prologue was written in early 2022 at the latest, as it generally takes six months or more for vinyl factories to press and package records. This was way before the world was shocked by GPT-3.5 in late 2022. So, there is no possible way she could asked an AI to write her prologue—unless someone at OpenAI infiltrated her management team to leak a copy of the record illegally.

Taylor’s writing revealed how AI detectors function: they are not "virtual police dogs" capable of sniffing out AI text. A more suitable description is that they operate more like primary school students comparing pre-determined patterns of "AI-generated" versus "human" content, looking for subtle patterns and deviations that don't actually prove authorship.

According to 2023 research, the mechanism of AI-generated text detectors is largely based on a simple assumption: human writing and AI-generated writing are distinct (Chakraborty et al., 2023). The researchers argue that these two forms of writing follow different statistical distributions. As they state, "As long as the two distributions are not exactly the same, which is rarely the case, detection will always be possible by collecting more samples" (p. 6). Essentially, while human and AI writing may appear similar in a single sentence, a larger sample pool will almost certainly reveal patterns that indicate authorship.

GPTZero, a popular AI detection service, further identifies **perplexity** and **burstiness** as the two main indicators that separate AI writing from human intellectual products (Barlow, 2024):

- **Perplexity:** Human writing tends to feature more surprising word choices, while chatbots typically output common, high-probability words and phrases. This makes human writing more "perplexed."
- **Burstiness:** Human writing features varied sentence lengths and structures, whereas AI tends to generate relatively uniform sentences, making the tone appear flat and steady. Therefore, human writing is considered more "bursty."

Such binary classification is unapologetically problematic. As noted by Bassett et al., "A persistent flaw in the use of AI detectors is the assumption that linguistic markers of AI and human-generated text are mutually exclusive" (2026, p.3). The corpora used to train these algorithms are usually scraped from the internet and consist mostly of native, fluent English text—think music reviews, blog posts, and news articles. As a consequence, AI-detector models are naturally biased against non-native English learners; these writers often lean toward simpler, more straightforward vocabulary and sentence structures to ensure their final product is grammatically accurate and clear.

Then there is the personal experience side of things. A considerable proportion of my students  rely heavily on translation services like Google Translate or DeepL, often because they lack the confidence to write in English from scratch, or to put it directly, being lazy af. This machine-translated text is almost always at risk of being labeled as AIGC, even though it isn't "human writing" in the traditional sense anyway.

The other issue is for those who have a distinct style of producing formal, academic-sounding text. In fact, this is where things start to get interesting. For centuries, students have been trained to produce academically structured essays that explain arguments in clear, organised ways. The standard advice is: always be clear and accurate; use signposting vocabulary to show the direction of your argument; and avoid using colloquialisms or slang. Ironically, this highly systematic writing designed specifically to deliver clarity and avoid errors, is almost certain to be flagged for a "lack of perplexity and burstiness" by AI detection algorithms.

This explains why the algorithm might think Taylor Swift's album prologues or an Apple press release were drafted by a chatbot: both materials exhibit lower levels of variation, focusing either on delivering a rhythmic, steady cadence or adhering to a standardised corporate format.

Accordingly, the accuracy of AI detectors has been the elephant in the room since day one. Despite being meticulously trained, these models showcase a considerable degree of instability. Some troublemakers even ran the Declaration of Independence through an AI detector, which returned an AI probability of 98.51%—with similar results for the Holy Bible (Cook, 2024). Research studying the efficacy of AI detectors revealed a diagnostic false-positive rate ranging anywhere from 0% to 100% (Elkhatat et al., 2023). A more recent study assessed detectors’ accuracy regarding health-related essays and reported a median misidentification rate of 27.2% for human-written work (Popkov & Barrett, 2025). Furthermore, Stanford University research revealed that AI detectors classified 61.22% of essays written by non-native English students as AI-generated (Liang et al., 2023), showcasing a worrisome bias against ESL students.

The answer is clear: AI detectors should not be used as the gold standard for identification in place of empirical methods. Even Turnitin admits this in the teeny-tiny notes of their AI reports, stating that it takes further scrutiny and human judgment: in conjunction with an organisation's specific policies, to determine if academic misconduct has occurred. But how did we get here? How did we enter such a knotty and horrid situation where students have to invest a massive amount of effort just to chase that "unlikely AI" result?

# Anxiolytic Painkiller

The emergence and proliferation of AI detection services is essentially a panic response from higher education and its associated industrial organizations. Following the disruption of large language models, institutions are desperately looking for a sense of "certainty" in a fearsome and wretched landscape.

Almost all major higher education institutions (HEIs) opted to stand against the technology after ChatGPT shocked the world. In February 2023, The University of Hong Kong (HKU) announced a temporary prohibition on Generative AI for academic-related practices, including coursework, classwork, and assessments (Yau & Chan, 2023). This was followed by a handful of Russell Group universities, including Oxbridge, explicitly stating that using Gen-AI is against academic integrity and protocols (Schifano, 2023).

Such a panic response is yet another portrayal of the moral panic people have been dealing with since the beginning of human history: just like when Socrates feared the invention of writing would stop people from memorising and cause them to become "hearers of many things and \[having] learned nothing." In his more in-depth research on moral panic, Cohen (1972, p.9) defined such a phenomenon as a chain of reactions:

>Societies appear to be subject, every now and then, to periods of moral panic. A condition, episode, person or group of persons emerges to become defined as a threat to societal values and interests; its nature is presented in a stylised and stereotypical fashion by the mass media; the moral barricades are manned by editors, bishops, politicians and other right-thinking people; socially accredited experts pronounce their diagnoses and solutions; ways of coping are evolved or (more often) resorted to; the condition then disappears, submerges or deteriorates and becomes more visible. 

The initial fear of Generative AI almost perfectly replicated this framework. Earliest media narratives were undeniably crisis-oriented, filled with hyperbolic, atrocious headlines selling anxiety. Like "abject terror" (Mitchell, 2022), or "a system that will usher in a cheater’s paradise and surely stymie critical thinking, innovation and progress" (Pohl, 2023). Abundant media reports claim that even the most astute professors find it tough to distinguish between AI and human-written essays, making it feel as if the entire higher education system is on the precipice and the traditional essay-centric assessment system is a house of cards. Consequently, it is quite thorny to say whether the disastrous tone of media coverage fuelled HEIs to make up their minds and sign off on AI bans, or vice-versa.

One thing that can be stated with certainty, however, is that the introduction of AI detectors relieves the anxiety and distress of HEIs as they look for certainty in a situation of agony. Various companies introduced and marketed their AI detection software, including OpenAI. On January 31, OpenAI launched its AI classifier and cited the service as a utility to "inform mitigations for false claims that AI-generated text was written by a human" (OpenAI, 2023). HEIs instantly began to adopt the service as a ray of hope. The University of Southern California (USC) mentioned the classifier alongside GPTZero in its Gen-AI Guideline as something teachers could explore (Crispen & Neuman, 2023). In practice, an "AI probability rate" is much more straightforward, effortless, and persuasive (on the surface) than empirical judgments, so professors chasing AI detection results is not surprising.

Interestingly, OpenAI took down the classifier in less than six months simply because it was inaccurate. However, this has not stopped other businesses from monetising AI text detection. Almost all university students have some experience with Turnitin, a California-based education giant providing essay submission, review toolkits, and plagiarism detection services to thousands of HEIs worldwide. In April 2023, Turnitin launched its own AI writing detection system (Caren, 2023) and opened a Pandora’s box of nightmares. The detector can be accessed by HEI staff through a range of Turnitin products, including Feedback Studio and Simcheck, reaching more than 10,700 HEIs and 62 million students.

The AI detector is not a gratis service for existing subscribers, unlike Apple Music’s lossless audio. Turnitin charges annual institutional subscriptions priced on a per-student basis; the AI detection feature is not included in the basic license but is a value-added service available with premium-tier plans. A report indicated that Turnitin's pricing is fairly vague and inconsistent: "companies charge different amounts to different institutions based on how much they expect the institutions are willing to pay" (Mathewson, 2025). The price gap in fees paid by different institutions for the same feature can be as much as double or more, and universities have to pay approximately an extra 50 cents per student to activate the AI detector.

Despite Turnitin being a private company (meaning we cannot access its financial reports like those of publicly traded businesses), forecasts suggest that the implementation of the AI detector has significantly fuelled its income growth, thanks to higher per-student licensing fees and improved customer retention/upsells during the post-ChatGPT panic. Independent estimates from Scara (2025) revealed Turnitin's revenue rose to $203 million in 2024—up 10% from $185 million in 2023—which aligns with the timeline of AI detector adoption.

The answer is pretty clear: Turnitin capitalised on the ChatGPT panic by launching an AI detection service that is not necessarily accurate, essentially monetising fear. HEIs' fear of Gen-AI and essays written by chatbots—which are almost indistinguishable—allowed the company to upsell AI writing indicators as a necessity rather than an add-on; just like that, they became richer. Don't forget we are living in a reality driven by an information and data economy, so every time a student uploads an essay to Turnitin, the company gets to cultivate more polished algorithms from that writing and reinforce its industry leadership (or monopoly, as it already is).

As for the students, they are ironically being forced to engage in a "reverse Turing test" just to prove they are not robots.

## What's Next?

The good news is that this is not the end of the world. Knowing that there are still institutions opting out of AI detection systems is such a relief. You see, despite people’s tendency to act in a state of absolute hysteria in response to the emergence of new technology, we need to learn to coexist with it—and this is exactly what we have done over the past hundreds of years. As seen in a report published by the Higher Education Policy Institute (HEPI), the infiltration rate of Gen-AI among UK students has reached 95%, meaning its use is almost universal. Over two-thirds of students state that AI literacy is essential and are calling for institutions to invest more in AI education. AI is not a "yes or no" problem; we have to learn how to adapt as AI continues to embed itself in more and more aspects of social practices.

Some universities have announced a full withdrawal from AI writing detectors. The University of Leeds (2026) has firmly made up its mind, stating "AI detection tools must not be used under any circumstances," noting potential data breaches, AI-training corpora issues, and accuracy problems. Alternatively, the university suggested a more responsible strategy for potential AI misuse: gathering evidence and submitting it to integrity officers.

Other universities have adopted a progressive approach to integrating AI by educating students to use it appropriately and responsibly (Weale, 2023). One trend is the deviation from conventional essay-based assessments. When I was at university, grades were almost certainly assessed by written responses, including essays, case studies, and annotated bibliographies. But I’ve seen many institutions incorporating more varied forms of assessments, such as presentations, podcasts, and portfolios. Apparently, these are designed to maximise "humanity" in homework, emphasising the process, authentic demonstrations of understanding, and skills that AI struggles to replicate; after all, I do not think we have the technology to digitally clone a human being at this point.

Certain institutions actually encourage students to use AI—not for writing an essay, but as a companion that helps with generating ideas, locating information, and structuring paragraphs. Professors are also granted autonomy to design their very own AI policies that fit the teaching goals of a specific module (Baker & Boland, 2026). Students are often required to submit an AI disclosure form along with their homework to let professors understand how the technology was used, helping HEIs prepare students for the future workforce through ethical and practical integration. Some universities have even implemented a "traffic light" system as a visual way to signal AI permission (University of Leeds, 2026): Red for no AI use permitted, Amber/Yellow for limited usage, and Green for encouraged use. This de-emphasises traditional essays in favor of diverse, process-oriented assessments while treating AI as a tool.

This shift is not surprising, as the ultimate fate of all technologies is either to be abandoned or gradually domesticated and integrated into daily life (Silverstone et al., 1992). Although Turnitin’s infamous detection system is still functioning and torturing millions of students—and my next client is likely to demand a reduction in their "AI probability" score—this melodramatic skit is undoubtedly coming to an end. The dynamics between AI detection systems and LLMs are essentially an arms race. Currently, AI technology is in a period of rapid development; the best AI model available at the start of the year is often the worst by the end. Every defensive update is met with a sophisticated offensive countermeasure in a never-ending cycle.

The question of detecting student AI use for plagiarism remains intricate and highly debatable. It is difficult to define exactly which uses of AI constitute plagiarism. A student might leave markdown formatting in their essay, almost certainly revealing AI usage, while another might generate all the arguments and structures via ChatGPT but write the final work by hand, concealing all AI footprints. I am confident that students in the future will not need to spend their nights fighting AI detectors just to prove their intellectual property is their own.

## The Extras

And at the very last, the implementation of AI detection services has  gave birth to something no one could have predicted: we have written manuals teaching you how to prompt AI chatbots to “humanise” your writing. These curated guides have word-by-word prompt samples that you can simply copy-paste into a chat box. There are also services monetising from desperate students, in which they can paste their essay into a text box, and the algorithm behind will help you to humanise the writing. Or if you are willing to pay more, you get to hire a real person to humanise your sentences.

Asking an AI to produce something that is considered "more human" than an actual human being—a highly sophisticated creature that requires carbohydrates, protein, and fat to survive—truly illustrates our post-modern reality, one in which "realness" has dissolved. When AI-modified text appears more natural than a student’s own words, we have to ask ourselves: has AI become so "humane" that it is now a simulacrum of a human being? If AI-generated text can trick another AI whose job is to identify AI, then how do we define the fundamental characteristics of human literature? 

Having a machine to judge humanity is absolutely by far the most pathetic thing ever happened to mankind.

##### FIN.
